card.intuition: Walk the list once, rewiring each node's next pointer to point backward at the previous node instead of forward.
card.analogy: A train reversing direction by uncoupling each car and re-coupling it behind the last car already reversed — one car at a time, no extra track needed.
when_to_use: In-place list reversal as a subroutine (reverse a sublist for reorder-list, palindrome check, etc.)
Reversing groups of k nodes at a time (k-group reversal)
Checking if a linked list is a palindrome (reverse the second half, compare)
gotchas: Save next = curr.next BEFORE rewiring curr.next — otherwise you lose the rest of the list
The three-pointer dance (prev, curr, next) must happen in the correct order: save next, rewire, advance
Recursive reversal has O(n) stack depth — iterative is always preferred for very long lists

exercise.prompt: Reverse a singly linked list in-place. Return the new head.
hints: curr.next should point to prev — the node just behind it in the reversed order
After rewiring, advance: prev becomes curr, curr becomes nxt (the saved original next)
exercise.test_code:
class ListNode:
    def __init__(self, val=0, next=None):
        self.val = val; self.next = next

def make_list(vals):
    d = ListNode(); c = d
    for v in vals: c.next = ListNode(v); c = c.next
    return d.next

def to_arr(head):
    r = []
    while head: r.append(head.val); head = head.next
    return r

assert to_arr(reverse_list(make_list([1,2,3,4,5]))) == [5,4,3,2,1]
assert to_arr(reverse_list(make_list([1,2])))        == [2,1]
assert to_arr(reverse_list(make_list([1])))          == [1]
assert reverse_list(None) is None
print('PASS')
